coredata.dc:title: Dy(III)-induced aggregation emission quenching effect of single-layered graphene quantum dots for selective detection of phosphate in the artificial wetlands
coredata.dc:creator: Chai, Shui Qin
He, Jia Hui
Zhan, Lei
Li, Yuan Fang
Li, Chun Mei
Huang, Cheng Zhi
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), prepared by one-step hydrothermal treatment of perylene-3,4,9,10-tetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA) and triethylamine (TEA), could be exfoliated or delaminated into single-layered graphene quantum dots (s-GQDs) with methanol for the first time, with fluorescence (FL) emission at 500 nm when excited at 417 nm. The s-GQDs, with more sufficient carboxyl groups on the surface than CQDs, could be induced to be aggregated by metal ion dysprosium (Dy3+), resulting in aggregation-induced emission quenching effect subsequently. However, the presence of phosphate (PO4 3-) destroys the Dy3+-induced aggregates of s-GQDs owing to the strong coordination between Dy3+ and PO4 3-, inducing the FL emission recovery of the s-GQDs and providing selective detection method of PO4 3- in the artificial wetlands with the linear range of 0.2–30 μM and determination limit of 0.1 μM (3σ).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Single-layered graphene quantum dots
s-GQDs-Dy3+ system
Phosphate detection

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of PO4 3- in the sample of artificial wetlands
Aggregation-disaggregation equilibrium of s-GQDs as mediated by Dy(III) and PO4 3-
Selective detection of PO4 3- based on the fluorescence recovery of s-GQDs-Dy3+ complex

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the as-prepared CQDs. (a) TEM/HRTEM images (inset: particle size distribution); (b,c) AFM images and the height profile; (d) The Raman spectra of CQDs and s-GQDs.
Characterization of the s-GQDs. (a,b) TEM/HRTEM images (inset: particle size distribution); (c,d) AFM images and the height profile.

(a) The FTIR spectra of the as-prepared CQDs and s-GQDs; (b,c) The XPS spectra of the as-prepared CQDs (b) and s-GQDs (c); (d) Normalized UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescent excitation and emission spectra of s-GQDs.

(a) Fluorescent spectra of the s-GQDs with the addition of various concentration of Dy3+ (0.0 μM, 1.0 μM, 2.0 μM, 6.0 μM, 10.0 μM, 16.0 μM, 20.0 μM, 26.0 μM, 30.0 μM, 40.0 μM, 50.0 μM, 60.0 μM, 70.0 μM). Inset: the relative fluorescence intensities (F/F 0) at 500 nm titrated with Dy3+ ions in Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4). EX: 417 nm, EM: 500 nm. The inset picture show the fluorescence quenching of the s-GQDs solutions under a UV-lamp. (b) F/F 0 in the presence of different kinds of metal ions; metal ions: 40.0 μM. F and F 0 represented the fluorescence intensity of s-GQDs in the presence and absence of metal ions, respectively. (c) The fluorescence quenching effect of Dy3+ on the as-prepared CQDs or s-GQDs. Dy3+, 40.0 μM.

TEM and AFM images of s-GQDs-Dy3+ complex in the absence or presence of PO4 3-. (a,c) Aggregated s-GQDs-Dy3+ in the absence of PO4 3- ions; (b,d) Dispersed s-GQDs in the presence of PO4 3- ions. s-GQDs, 0.05 mg/mL; Dy3+, 40.0 μM; PO4 3-, 80 μM.

(a) Zeta potential of s-GQDs in the absence and presence of Dy3+ or PO4 3-; (b) Time resolved decay of the s-GQDs in the presence of Dy3+ and PO4 3- ions in Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4). Dy3+, 40.0 μM; PO4 3-, 80.0 μM.

(a) Fluorescence recovery obtained from the addition of 0.2–100.0 μM PO4 3- to the s-GQDs-Dy3+ solution; (b) The plot of the value of (F/F 0) at 500 nm versus the various concentration of PO4 3- ions in Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4). F and F 0 represented the fluorescence intensity of s-GQDs-Dy3+ in the presence and absence of PO4 3-, respectively. The inset showed the responses of the s-GQDs-Dy3+ to PO4 3- at low concentrations (0.2–30.0 μM), R2 = 0.9987. s-GQDs, 0.05 mg/mL; Dy3+, 40.0 μM.

The selectivity of PO4 3- detection by using s-GQDs-Dy3+ system. The columns represent: PO4 3-; S2-; F-; Br-; Cl-; I-; CH3COO-; IO3 -; NO3 -; SO3 2-; S2O3 2-; S2O8 2-; SCN-; Glu; Ser; Ca2+; K+; Mg2+; Cd2+; Mn2+; Ni2+; Concentrations: PO4 3-, 40.0 μM; all the other anions, 400 μM. All data were collected at 500 nm.
Illustration of synthesis of s-GQDs by exfoliating the as-prepared CQDs with methanol (a) and the highly selective detection of PO4 3- by using s-GQDs-Dy3+ strategy (b).
Measurement results of PO4 3- in the artificial wetlands.

head.ce:title: Dy(III)-induced aggregation emission quenching effect of single-layered graphene quantum dots for selective detection of phosphate in the artificial wetlands

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescence and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Biomedical Analysis, Chongqing Science & Technology Commission, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Key Laboratory of Biomedical Analysis, Chongqing Science & Technology Commission, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), prepared by one-step hydrothermal treatment of perylene-3,4,9,10-tetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA) and triethylamine (TEA), could be exfoliated or delaminated into single-layered graphene quantum dots (s-GQDs) with methanol for the first time, with fluorescence (FL) emission at 500 nm when excited at 417 nm. The s-GQDs, with more sufficient carboxyl groups on the surface than CQDs, could be induced to be aggregated by metal ion dysprosium (Dy3+), resulting in aggregation-induced emission quenching effect subsequently. However, the presence of phosphate (PO4 3-) destroys the Dy3+-induced aggregates of s-GQDs owing to the strong coordination between Dy3+ and PO4 3-, inducing the FL emission recovery of the s-GQDs and providing selective detection method of PO4 3- in the artificial wetlands with the linear range of 0.2–30 μM and determination limit of 0.1 μM (3σ).

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was firstly reported to be exfoliated or delaminated into single-layered graphene quantum dots (s-GQDs) by mixing with methanol followed by evaporating. The s-GQDs with sufficient carboxyl groups on the surface could specifically be induced by metal ion dysprosium (Dy3+) to form aggregates, resulting in aggregation-induced emission quenching effect. However, the presence of phosphate (PO4 3-) destroyed the Dy3+-induced aggregates of s-GQDs, inducing the FL emission recovery of the s-GQDs, which can be used for sensitive and selective detection of PO4 3-.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a new type of carbon-based nanomaterial, have been highly concerned owing to their intriguing properties, such as their tunable photoluminescence, photostability, water solubility, great environmental sustainability, low production cost, low toxicity as well as biocompatibility [1–6]. These properties are superior to semiconductor quantum dots and the organic dyes fluorescence probes, which enable GQDs to be a great candidate of optical probes for chemical analysis [7], sensing [8] and bioimaging [9]. Inspired by these outstanding properties and potential applications, increasingly researchers has focused on the synthesis of GQDs, including mechanical exfoliation [10–12], electrochemical approach [13], cutting large graphene sheets via a hydrothermal route [14] and carbonizing suitable organic precursors [15]. However, current GQDs produced by the above methods are mostly multilayered, which is not favorable for accurate chemical structure observation and their photoluminescent properties study. Therefore, it is highly desirable to synthesize single-layered GQDs (s-GQDs), which might response highly owing to the rich surface groups on both sides of the s-GQD sheets.

So far, the preparation of s-GQDs with a uniform size distribution is one of the biggest obstacles in field of graphene research. Our research group has reported a novel method for the synthesis of s-GQDs through an efficient self-exothermic reaction at room temperature [16], which present great properties such as good stability and water solubility. In this work, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were first prepared by one-step hydrothermal treatment of perylene-3,4,9,10-tetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA) and triethylamine (TEA), then we found s-GQDs could be easily obtained by dissolving the as-prepared CQDs into 50% methanol and then evaporating off methanol, which is very fascinating. Moreover, it is known that metal ion dysprosium (Dy3+), which compounds exhibit distinct white light emissions and are often applied for white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) [17], appears to coordinate with the oxygen-donor atoms from carboxylate groups [18]. Thus, Dy3+ could react to the s-GQDs containing a large amount of carboxylate groups, resulting in aggregation-induced emission quenching effect, which is not observed for the precursor CQDs.

Furthermore, studies have found Dy3+ is the most prominent phospho-affinity element from the various rare earth ions [19]. Phosphorus is an indispensable element in the growth of plants and animals, and inputs of it have always been considered necessary to sustain profitable crops and animal production. Phosphate (PO4 3-), as one of the major forms of phosphorus in natural waters, may be sometimes a limiting factor and its control is of prime necessity in reducing the accelerated eutrophication of fresh waters. In recent years, various fluorescence probes have been developed to detect PO4 3- specifically, including organic dyes [20], semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [21] and carbon dots (CDs) [22,23]. They are excellent for PO4 3- sensing, but still limited by the range of response and complicated modification processes. Therefore, it is necessary to develop simple, economical methods for rapid and sensitive determination of PO4 3-. In this work, we apply the aggregation structures of Dy3+-induced s-GQDs for sensitive detection of PO4 3- in complicate matrix by utilizing the competition between PO4 3- and s-GQDs for Dy3+ ions on the basis of off-on fluorescence during the aggregation-disaggregation equilibrium of s-GQDs ( Scheme 1).

Dysprosium sulfate (Dy2(SO4)3, 99.99%) was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China), and its stock solution of 5.0 mM was prepared and stored at 4 °C. Sodium phosphate (Na3PO4) was commercially available from Chongqing Chemical Reagents Co., (Chongqing, China) and 5.0 mM of stock solution was prepared. The working solutions were prepared via diluting appropriately the stock solutions. A 10.0 mM Tris-HCl solution containing 0.15 M NaCl was disposed and adjusted in advance to control the acidity and adjust the ionic strength. All chemicals from commercial sources were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Ultra-pure water (18.2 MΩ) was produced from a Millipore water purification system (Milli-Q, Millipore, Billerica, USA) and used for the preparation of all solutions.

All fluorescence spectra were recorded with a Hitachi F-2500 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained with a Tecnai G2 F20 field emission transmission electron microscope (FEI, USA). The thickness distribution of CQDs or s-GQDs was characterized using a Dimension Icon Scan Asyst atomic force microscope (Bruker Co.). The Raman spectrum on the AgNPs substrate was performed on a LabRAM HR800 Laser confocal Raman spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France). Elemental and functional group analyses were obtained using an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc.) and a Hitachi FTIR-8400S Fourier Transform Infrared spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan). The FL-TCSPC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France) was used to measure the fluorescence life time. Zeta potentials were recorded with a Zetasizer Nano ZS System (ZEN3600, Malvern, UK). The pH values of the aqueous solutions were measure using a Fangzhong pHS-3C digital pH meter (Chengdu, China).

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared by one-step hydrothermal treatment according to our previous procedures [24]. Briefly, 0.26 g perylene-3,4,9,10-tetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA) and 1 mL triethylamine (TEA) was dissolved into 4.0 mL double distilled water, and 5.0 mL of the mixture was transferred in a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainlesss steel autoclave and then heated to 220 °C for 48 h. The autoclave cooling down naturally, the resultants was filtered with 0.22 µm BIOSHARP membrane filters to remove large particles, and transparent aqueous solution was then subjected to dialysis (M w = 1000 Da) for 48 h to eliminate the residual PTCDA and TEA. Finally, the purified CQDs were freeze-dried under vacuum (−80 °C) to obtain solid powder. Importantly, the s-GQDs was obtained by mixing vigorously the as-prepared CQDs solution (0.5 mg/mL) with a same volume of methanol for a few minutes and then evaporating off methanol at 80 °C.

In a typical run, 50.0 μL 10 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4) and 50.0 μL 0.5 mg/mL s-GQDs were added into a 1.5 mL EP tube, followed by the addition of 50.0 μL of different concentrations of Dy3+ ion solution. The mixture was blended thoroughly, and then diluted to 0.5 mL with double distilled water. After incubation for 30 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectrum was recorded with the excitation wavelength at 417 nm and emission wavelength at 500 nm. The final concentration of Dy3+ was kept at 40.0 μM. Subsequently, various concentrations of PO4 3- were added into the s-GQDs-Dy3+ solution, and the gradual fluorescence recovery of s-GQDs was measured following the above condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of PO4 3- in the sample of artificial wetlands
ce:section.ce:para: The Artificial Wetlands (AWs) water samples were collected from the effluent exit of one Artificial Wetlands system located in the Southwest University, China. All the AWs water samples were filtered through 0.22 µm membrane filters.

The TEM image ( Fig. 1a) displayed that the as-prepared CQDs were uniformly dispersed, and had a relatively narrow size distribution between 2.3 nm and 5.0 nm with an average diameter of 3.2 nm. The HRTEM images (inset of Fig. 1a) showed the lattice spacings of 0.33 nm ([002] facet) and 0.24 nm ([100] facet), which correspond to the between-planes and in-plane lattice spacings of graphitic carbon, respectively [25,26]. The AFM image (Fig. 1b, c) depicted that the height of the CQDs varied mostly from 1.6 nm to 2.5 nm, indicating that CQDs consisted of 4–6 layers of graphene.

It has been reported that layered materials can adsorb guest molecules into the spacing between layers [27]. Methanol is a small, polar molecule, the intercalation of which increases the layer spacing, weakening the interlayer adhesion and reducing the energy barrier to exfoliation. Subsequent treatment such as thermal shock [28] in a liquid completes the exfoliation process. In our experiment, we found that the as-prepared CQDs could be easily exfoliated or delaminated into s-GQDs with the treatment of methanol. This production of extremely thin s-GQDs from the as-prepared CQDs can be considered as exfoliation or delamination. To demonstrate the formation of s-GQDs, a series of experiments had been carried out. The TEM image ( Fig. 2a) showed that the average diameter of s-GQDs was about 2.1 nm, and the HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 2b) clearly indicated the crystallinity of the s-GQDs, with just one lattice spacing of 0.23 nm which is attributed to the (100) facet lattice fringe of graphene [26]. The height profile of AFM measurements (Fig. 2c,d) suggested that most of the s-GQDs was a single layer graphene sheet of about 0.7 nm. Raman spectral measurements (Fig. 1d) obtained on the Ag substrate (excited by a 532 nm laser) confirmed that there were two narrow peaks G-line at around 1574 cm−1 and D-line at around 1372 cm−1, which correspond respectively to sp2 hybridized carbon and sp3 carbon in the as-prepared s-GQDs and the precursor CQDs. Furthermore, the I
G
/I
D ratio increased from 1.05 to 1.26, indicating that s-GQDs with enhanced sp2 hybridized carbon are highly crystalline and have a similar structure to graphite [29,30].

In order to understand the composition transformation from CQDs to s-GQDs, the FTIR spectra and XPS were measured. The FTIR spectra ( Fig. 3a) of s-GQDs, similar to that of CQDs, had the characteristic absorption bands of O-H vibrations and N-H around 3435 cm−1, C-H stretching vibrations at 2927 cm−1 and 2980 cm−1, C=O bonds stretching vibrations around 1636 cm−1, C-N bonds stretching vibrations around 1400 cm−1 and C-O stretching vibrations at 1066 cm−1 [25,31,32]. Both the XPS full scan spectrum of the precursor CQDs (Fig. 3b) and the s-GQDs (Fig. 3c) presented three dominant peaks of carbon (C 1s, 285 eV), nitrogen (N 1s, 400 eV) and oxygen (O 1 s, 533 eV) [8,9]. It is noticeable that the oxygen content of the as-prepared s-GQDs was 47.5%, which was obviously higher than that of the precursor CQDs (24.8%). High-resolution C 1s spectra of the precursor CQDs (Fig. S1a) and the s-GQDs (Fig. S1d) showed three peaks at 284.8, 286.3 and 287.8 eV, corresponding to sp2 carbon (C-C), sp3 carbon (C-O/C-N) and carbonyl carbons (C=O), respectively [8,24,33]. High-resolution N 1s spectra of the precursor CQDs (Fig. S1b) and the s-GQDs (Fig. S1e) showed three peaks at 399.6, 401.2 and 402.3 eV, which were attributed to pyrrolic N (C-N-C) and graphitic N or N-H bands, respectively [25,34]. The bands in O 1 s spectra (Fig. S1c,f) presented two peaks at 531.7 and 532.6 eV, corresponding to C=O and C-O, respectively [24]. The above results indicated that, even though there was no difference in the species of the peaks for CQDs and s-GQDs, the total oxygen content in the XPS full scan spectrum, which was mainly attributed to carboxylate group, was increased when as-prepared CQDs were exfoliated or delaminated into s-GQDs with the treatment of methanol. In addition, the as-prepared s-GQDs had similar absorption and emission abilities to the CQDs. As shown in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 3d), the s-GQDs had a typical absorption bands characterized at 254 nm due to the π→π* transition of C=C and two small bands at 418 nm and 443 nm owing to the n→π * transition of the surface groups. The maximum emission peak was located at 500 nm with a maximum excitation at 417 nm, which was consistent with the absorption features centered at 418 nm. The fluorescence intensity of s-GQDs was pH-independent in the Tris-HCl pH range 6.5–9.5 (Fig. S2), which was consistent with that of the as-prepared CQDs as reported in the previous research [24].

Dy3+, one of the important lanthanide metal ions, displays good affinity to the oxygen-donor atoms from carboxylate groups [18], and thus can coordinate with the abundant carboxylate group on the surface of s-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 4a, with the addition of Dy3+ progressively to the s-GQDs under optimized conditions, fluorescence quenching gradually occurred along with an increase in the concentration of Dy3+ which was visible to the naked-eye under the UV-illumination (inset photograph in Fig. 4a). When the concentration of Dy3+ was above 30.0 μM, the fluorescence intensity of the s-GQDs was almost completely turned off and then reached equilibrium. Finally, 40.0 μM Dy3+ was chosen as the optimum concentration for the subsequent experiments to achieve better stability. In addition, it is a matter of essential for Dy3+ to specially coordinate with s-GQDs. Fig. 4b showed that Dy3+ induced an obviously decrease in the FL intensity of s-GQDs while the other metal ions including Ba2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Hg2+ caused neglectable effect on the FL intensity. Although the middle lanthanides (Sm3+, Eu3+, Gd3+, Tb3+) plus Yb3+, Fe3+, Al3+ showed binding preference for the s-GQDs, the binding ability of these metal ions for carboxyl-containing s-GQDs was remarkably weaker than Dy3+. The lower F/F 0 ratio in the presence and absence of Dy3+ demonstrated the high specificity of s-GQDs to Dy3+.

It was interesting that the FL intensity of s-GQDs was obviously quenched upon addition of 40.0 μM Dy3+, while the precursor CQDs was not quenched at the same condition (Fig. 4c). Considering that the carboxylate group content of s-GQDs was higher than that of the precursor CQDs, it is easier for s-GQDs to react with Dy3+ ions than the precursor CQDs. Moreover, different kinds of s-GQDs obtained by the reaction of CQDs with various volume percent of methanol (0.1–50%) exhibited almost the same fluorescence intensity (Fig. S3). However, when adding the same amount of Dy3+, the fluorescence of s-GQDs got gradually quenched with the increasing volume percent of methanol in the range of 0.1–50% (Fig. S4), indicating that the more and more s-GQDs were transformed from CQDs with the increasing amount of methanol. When the volume percent of methanol was larger than 50%, the aggregation of s-GQDs was visible. Thus, 50% methanol was used to exfoliate the precursor CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aggregation-disaggregation equilibrium of s-GQDs as mediated by Dy(III) and PO4 3-
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that Dy3+ is a new ideal choice in phospho-affinity materials [19]. In our work, we found the Dy3+-induced aggregation emission quenching effect could be restrained in the presence of PO4 3- since Dy3+ displayed higher affinity with oxygen-donor atoms from PO4 3- than that with the ones from the carboxylate groups on the surface of s-GQDs. To investigate the mechanism of aggregation-disaggregation of s-GQDs, the morphological evolution process was observed by TEM and HRTEM. As identified clearly by the TEM image, when Dy3+ was added, the monodispersed s-GQDs with the diameter range of 1.3–3.4 nm (Fig. 2a,b) got aggregated into amorphous masses ( Fig. 5a), which subsequently got dispersed in the presence of PO4 3- (Fig. 5b), indicating that PO4 3- destroys the Dy3+-induced aggregates of s-GQDs due to its competition for Dy3+ ions with s-GQDs. To further verify this point, AFM imaging was also measured. The thickness of s-GQDs was increased from approximately 0.7 nm to about 4.0 nm with the addition of Dy3+ (Fig. 2c,d, Fig. 5c), providing the Dy3+ -induced s-GQDs aggregation, which disaggregated with the subsequent addition of PO4 3- with the thickness of 1.9 nm (Fig. 5d). Comparatively, the precursor CQDs, containing less carboxyl groups than s-GQDs on their surfaces, still appeared monodispersed even in the presence of 40.0 μM Dy3+ (Fig. S5a), and its thickness was approximately 1.8 nm (Fig. S5b,c), indicating that the CQDs couldn’t be induced to aggregate into amorphous masses by Dy3+ as that of s-GQDs. Furthermore, the zeta potential of the s-GQDs in aqueous solution was −25.6 mV, while the negative charge turned into positive 3.01 mV once reacting with Dy3+ ( Fig. 6a). In contrast, further addition of PO4 3- returned to the negative charge again (−9.6 mV). In addition, there wasn’t obvious change of the fluorescence lifetime for the s-GQDs both in the absence and presence of Dy3+ or PO4 3-, suggesting that no energy transfer process happened (Fig. 6b).

Dy3+ coordinates with the oxygen-donor atoms from carboxylate groups [18] and also interacts with the negative s-GQDs through electrostatic interaction, thus acting as a bridge to link neighboring s-GQDs, resulting in the monodispersed s-GQDs aggregate into amorphous masses (Fig. 5a). However, the oxygen-donor atoms originated from phosphates exhibit higher affinity to Dy3+ ions than that from carboxylate groups, concluding that the introduction of PO4 3- disrupted the coordination and electro-static interaction between the s-GQDs and Dy3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective detection of PO4 3- based on the fluorescence recovery of s-GQDs-Dy3+ complex
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the fluorescence recovery of s-GQDs-Dy3+ complex, an off–on fluorescence sensing strategy was performed for the determination of PO4 3- based on the competition interaction between s-GQDs and PO4 3- for Dy3+. The optimization of pH in Tris-HCl buffer showed that, upon the addition of Dy3+, the FL intensity of the s-GQDs at 500 nm was quenched obviously in the range of pH 6.5–7.5, while the quenching was incompletely in the range of pH 7.5–9.5 (Fig. S6). As one of the hard acid, the Dy3+ ions could form insoluble hydrated oxide by complexing with the hard base OH - . Therefore, the s-GQDs couldn’t coordinate with the Dy3+ ions, resulting in the incomplete fluorescence quenching. Considering that the s-GQDs-Dy3+ complex was pH-dependent, pH 7.4 of Tris-HCl buffer was used in the subsequent experiments. Then we found that the FL intensity of the s-GQDs-Dy3+ complex was gradually recovered with the increasing concentration of PO4 3- ( Fig. 7a). Meanwhile, the solution color gradually turned brighter upon the addition of PO4 3- under the irradiation of UV light, indicating that the fluorescence recovery process could be visualized by naked eyes (inset photograph in Fig. 7a). The FL intensity ratio (F/F 0) of the s-GQDs-Dy3+ at 500 nm displayed good linearity with the concentration of PO4 3- in the range of 0.2–30.0 μM with the detection limit of 0.1 μM (3σ) (Fig. 7b).

To evaluate the selectivity of the s-GQDs-Dy3+ complex, we investigated the fluorescence response of s-GQDs-Dy3+ complex to different anions including carboxylic acids and some cations in aqueous (Tris-HCl buffer at pH 7.4) solutions. Fig. 8 showed that PO4 3- had unique recovery efficiency only in the presence of 40.0 μM while no change even the tolerant concentration of other anions was up to 10-folds of PO4 3- . The result verified that s-GQDs-Dy3+ complex was highly selective to PO4 3- .

PO4 3- is considered to be the main component of the nutrition chain of aquatic microorganisms, and it is often presented as an indicator of organic pollution in bodies of water. Therefore, we applied the fluorescence probe to detect PO4 3- in artificial wetlands. The recoveries study ( Table 1) were used to evaluate the accuracy of our proposed method, which was carried out on the samples spiked with standard PO4 3- solutions (4.0, 9.0 and 19.0 μM). The recovery over the range of 97.8–102.2% with the RSD values no more than 6% was acceptable for the detections of PO4 3- in such kind of complicated sample, suggesting that the proposed method does not suffer from interference effects in complex matrices.

In summary, we have proposed a novel strategy to prepare s-GQDs through methanol exfoliating or delaminating from the as-prepared CQDs, which offers a new idea to acquire s-GQDs by utilizing small molecule to exfoliate the existing CQDs or GQDs. As far as we know, this is the first report about the transformation of CQDs to GQDs with the assistance of solvent. The as-prepared s-GQDs, with more sufficient carboxyl groups on the surface than the precursor, could be induced to aggregate by lanthanide metal ion Dy3+ through the coordination and electrostatic interaction, resulting in aggregation-induced emission quenching effect. Furthermore, the fluorescence of s-GQDs-Dy3+ complex could be recovered upon the addition of PO4 3-, which displayed higher affinity to Dy3+ than s-GQDs. Therefore, an off-on fluorescent strategy was proposed and successfully applied for the detection of PO4 3- in the complexity of artificial wetlands samples. This approach is cost-effective, facile, label-free and has high reproducibility. These excellent features present a broad application prospect for detection of PO4 3- in different kinds of water samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, Grant No. 21535006, No. 21705131).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.12.032.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ram-scale synthesis of single-crystalline graphene quantum dots with superior optical properties
Deep ultraviolet photoluminescence of water-soluble self-passivated gaphene quantum dots
Unique approach to develop carbon dot-based nanohybrid near-infrared ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the detection of mercury ions
Facile synthesis of water-soluble, highly fluorescent graphene quantum dots as a robust biological label for stem cells
A low-temperature solid-phase method to synthesize highly fluorescent carbon nitride dots with tunable emission
Functional preserving carbon dots-based fluorescent probe for mercury (II) ions sensing in herbal medicines via coordination and electron transfer
A general quantitative pH sensor developed with dicyandiamide N-doped high quantum yield graphene quantum dots
Synthesis of nitrogen-doping carbon dots with different photoluminescence properties by controlling the surface states
